results.translation:
Now we shall expound the Vimana [systematic measure and classification] of Rasas [tastes/flavors].

There are six Rasas [tastes]: Madhura [sweet], Amla [sour], Lavaṇa [saline/salty], Kaṭu [pungent], Tikta [bitter], and Kaṣāya [astringent].

Because they are constituted by the five Mahābhūtas [great elements], the Rasas are themselves modifications of those five great elements. The Mahābhūtas are: Ākāśa [space/ether], Vāyu [air/wind], Agni [fire], Āpas [water], and Pṛthivī [earth].

Because Rasas are always substrate-dependent — that is, they inhere in material substances [dravya] — the substance itself is primary. A Rasa is never found apart from a substance; no Rasa is perceived without a material vehicle.

Rasas carry qualities [guṇas] such as: unctuous [snigdha] or dry [rūkṣa], hot [uṣṇa] or cold [śīta], heavy [guru] or light [laghu], stable [sthira] or mobile [sara], and so forth.

**Madhura [Sweet] — arising from the union of Pṛthivī [earth] and Āpas [water]:**
Pacifies Vāta and Pitta [the wind and fire humors]. Promotes Ojas [vital essence]. Is nourishing [bṛṃhaṇa] and pleasurable [prīṇana]. Produces satiation [tṛpti].

**Amla [Sour] — arising from the union of Pṛthivī [earth] and Agni [fire]:**
Pacifies Vāta. Kindles digestive fire [dīpana]. Promotes digestion [pācana]. Is cordial [hṛdya, agreeable to the heart]. May increase Pitta and Kapha [if used in excess].

**Lavaṇa [Saline/Salty] — arising from the union of Āpas [water] and Agni [fire]:**
Pacifies Vāta. Promotes sweating [svedana]. Promotes purgation/splitting [bhedana]. Kindles digestive fire [dīpana]. May increase Pitta and Kapha.

**Kaṭu [Pungent] — arising from the union of Vāyu [air] and Agni [fire]:**
May increase Kapha and Vāta [if used in excess]. Promotes digestion [pācana]. Kindles digestive fire [dīpana]. Cleanses [śodhana]. In proper measure, pacifies Pitta.

**Tikta [Bitter] — arising from the union of Vāyu [air] and Ākāśa [space]:**
Subdues Pitta and Kapha. Kindles digestive fire [dīpana]. Suppresses thirst [tṛṣṇānigrahī]. Alleviates fever [jvarahara]. Destroys parasites/worms [kṛmighna].

**Kaṣāya [Astringent] — arising from the union of Vāyu [air] and Pṛthivī [earth]:**
Subdues Pitta and Kapha. Is drying/absorbing [śoṣaṇa]. Promotes tissue-joining/wound healing [sandhanakārī]. Is styptic/arresting [stambhana].

entities_mentioned.name: Madhura rasa (sweet taste)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Represented by substances such as sugarcane (Saccharum officinarum), milk, ghee, licorice (Glycyrrhiza glabra), dates (Phoenix dactylifera)
entities_mentioned.notes: A class of substances rather than a single plant. Madhura rasa is associated with Pṛthivī and Āpas elements. Therapeutically used to pacify Vāta and Pitta, build Ojas, and promote anabolism.

entities_mentioned.name: Amla rasa (sour taste)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Represented by Āmalakī (Phyllanthus emblica / Emblica officinalis), tamarind (Tamarindus indica), citrus fruits, fermented preparations
entities_mentioned.notes: Amla rasa arises from Pṛthivī and Agni. Its digestive-stimulant and Vāta-pacifying properties are well-documented in classical Ayurvedic pharmacology.

entities_mentioned.name: Lavaṇa rasa (saline/salty taste)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Represented by rock salt (Saindhava lavaṇa / Halite, NaCl), sea salt, black salt (Kala namak)
entities_mentioned.notes: Lavaṇa rasa is constituted by Āpas and Agni. Its bhedana (splitting/purgative) and svedana (diaphoretic) properties reflect its use in stimulating downward movements and sweating.

entities_mentioned.name: Kaṭu rasa (pungent taste)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Represented by black pepper (Piper nigrum), long pepper (Piper longum), ginger (Zingiber officinale), mustard (Brassica spp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Kaṭu rasa is associated with Vāyu and Agni. Strongly digestive and cleansing; its aggravation of Kapha and Vāta in excess is a classical caution.

entities_mentioned.name: Tikta rasa (bitter taste)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Represented by Neem (Azadirachta indica), Guduchi/Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia), Kalamegha (Andrographis paniculata), Kirātatikta (Swertia chirata)
entities_mentioned.notes: Tikta rasa constituted by Vāyu and Ākāśa. Its kṛmighna (antiparasitic) and jvarahara (antipyretic) properties correspond well to the broad antimicrobial activity documented for bitter Ayurvedic herbs.

entities_mentioned.name: Kaṣāya rasa (astringent taste)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Represented by Arjuna (Terminalia arjuna), Lodhra (Symplocos racemosa), Haritakī (Terminalia chebula), Dhātakī (Woodfordia fruticosa)
entities_mentioned.notes: Kaṣāya rasa is constituted by Vāyu and Pṛthivī. Its stambhana (styptic/hemostatic) and sandhanakārī (wound-healing, tissue-uniting) properties are consistent with the high tannin content of its representative plants.

entities_mentioned.name: Ṣaḍ rasāḥ (six tastes)
entities_mentioned.notes: The sixfold classification of taste is a foundational organizing principle of Ayurvedic pharmacology. Each taste is understood as both a sensory quality and a pharmacodynamic category, predicting drug action (karma) on the three doṣas (Vāta, Pitta, Kapha).

entities_mentioned.name: Mahābhūtas (five great elements)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ākāśa (space), Vāyu (air), Agni (fire), Āpas (water), Pṛthivī (earth). These are not empirical elements but ontological categories constituting all material reality. Each rasa is understood as a binary combination of two elements, the dominant pairing governing its pharmacological character.

entities_mentioned.name: Ojas
entities_mentioned.notes: Ojas is the refined essence of all seven dhātus (bodily tissues), associated with immunity, vitality, and mental clarity. Madhura rasa is said to specifically promote Ojas (ojovardhana).

entities_mentioned.name: Doṣas (Vāta, Pitta, Kapha)
entities_mentioned.notes: The three humors or biological regulatory forces central to Ayurvedic physiology and pathology. This chapter frames each rasa's therapeutic value primarily in terms of which doṣas it pacifies or exacerbates.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text provided is a scholarly reconstruction/paraphrase of Vi.1 content rather than a preserved verbatim manuscript passage; the Sanskrit given follows classical Charaka style and standard Vimana Sthana vocabulary and is treated as authoritative for this entry.
2. 'Rasa Vimana' (Vi.1) is the first chapter of the Vimana Sthana ('Section on Measurement/Proportions'), one of the eight major divisions of the Charaka Samhita. The chapter establishes the theoretical basis for taste-based pharmacology.
3. The elemental assignments (e.g., Madhura = Pṛthivī + Āpas) are standard across Ayurvedic commentarial tradition (cf. Chakrapāṇidatta's Āyurvedadīpikā).
4. The statement 'na hi dravyaṃ vinā raso dṛśyate' ('no rasa is perceived without a material substance') is a philosophically significant anti-Mīmāṃsā position asserting that taste inheres in substance, not in a free-floating universal — an important point for Ayurvedic epistemology.
5. The phrase 'pramāṇena' appended to Kaṭu's relation to Pitta ('pacifies Pitta in proper measure') reflects the classical Ayurvedic caveat that dose and context modulate doshic effects — excess of any rasa can disturb the doṣa it normally subdues.
6. Modern identifications for each rasa class are representative exemplars, not exhaustive lists. Individual dravyas are classified by primary rasa but may carry secondary and post-digestive tastes (anurasa, vipāka) that further nuance their action.

results.title_inferred: Trividhakukshi Vimana: The Three Sections of the Alimentary Tract and Dietary Measurement
results.original_text: Vi.2 — Trividhakukshi Vimana (Vimana Sthana, Chapter 2): Treatment of the three divisions of the stomach/abdomen (kukshi), the concept of proper dietary quantity (aharamatra), the role of agni (digestive fire), and the effects of under-eating, over-eating, and proper eating on health. Key concepts: trikukshi (three sections of the abdomen), ahara (food/diet), matra (measure/quantity), agni (digestive fire), vata, pitta, kapha (the three dosas), ama (undigested metabolic waste), kosta (alimentary tract), deha (body), bala (strength), ayu (longevity), varna (complexion), utsaha (vitality), upasthambha (supportive pillars of life: ahara, nidra, brahmacharya).
results.translation:
Chapter 2 of the Vimana Sthana of the Charaka Samhita addresses the concept of the three divisions of the abdomen (trikukshi) and the proper measurement of food (aharamatra) as a cornerstone of health and disease prevention.

The chapter opens by establishing that the body's abdominal cavity (kukshi) is divided into three sections: the upper, middle, and lower thirds. The upper third accommodates kapha (the phlegmatic humor), the middle third accommodates pitta (the bilious humor), and the lower third accommodates vata (the wind humor). Proper nutrition sustains all three sections in balance.

The text then elaborates extensively on the concept of aharamatra — the proper quantity of food to be consumed at a single meal. The stomach (amashaya) is conceived as having a capacity that should ideally be filled in three equal parts: one-third solid food, one-third liquid, and one-third left empty to accommodate the movement of the dosas during digestion. This tripartite division mirrors the trikukshi schema.

Agni (the digestive fire, analogous to metabolic capacity) is described as the root of all physiological processes. When food is consumed in the correct measure (samyak matra), agni processes it efficiently, producing the finest essence (sara) that nourishes all bodily tissues (dhatu: rasa, rakta, mamsa, meda, asthi, majja, shukra) in sequential refinement. The byproducts (mala: urine, feces, sweat) are appropriately eliminated.

The chapter identifies three pathological states arising from improper food quantity:
1. Atimatra (excess eating / overeating): The excessive quantity overwhelms agni, leading to its suppression (agnimandya). Undigested food (ama) accumulates, obstructing the channels (srotas). This produces heaviness (gaurava), drowsiness (tandra), lassitude, impaired digestion, swelling of the abdomen, and in chronic cases, diseases of all three dosas simultaneously. Overeating is described as a primary cause of death and disease.
2. Hinamatra (under-eating / insufficient food): Insufficient food fails to sustain agni, leading to depletion of the dhatus sequentially. This causes emaciation (karshya), weakness (daurbalya), loss of complexion (varna-kshaya), impotence, diminished sensory acuity, mental instability, and susceptibility to vata disorders.
3. Samyak matra (proper measure): Food consumed in the correct quantity kindles agni without overwhelming it, is digested within the proper time frame (generally described as by the time one feels genuinely hungry again), nourishes all dhatus, maintains the dosas in equilibrium, promotes strength (bala), longevity (ayu), good complexion (varna), vitality (utsaha), and happiness (sukha).

The text enumerates the three great supportive pillars of life (trayopasthambha): ahara (diet), nidra (sleep), and brahmacharya (regulated sexual conduct/continence). Of these, ahara is treated as the foremost, as it is the direct material basis of the body and its sustenance.

The chapter further discusses how the quantity of food appropriate for an individual varies according to their constitution (prakriti), the strength of their agni (which may be tikshna/sharp, manda/dull, or sama/balanced), the nature of the food itself (guru/heavy to digest, laghu/light to digest), the season (ritu), the individual's age, and their habitual diet (satmya). What is a proper measure for one person may be excessive or insufficient for another; the physician must therefore individualize dietary prescriptions.

Heavy foods (guru ahara) — such as urad dal (Vigna mungo / masha), milk, meat of aquatic animals, sugarcane products, and new grain — require smaller quantities to satisfy without burdening agni. Light foods (laghu ahara) — such as mudga (green gram, Vigna radiata), shali rice (a fine variety of Oryza sativa), and most vegetables — may be consumed in larger quantities. The text notes that even a heavy food, if consumed in small quantity, becomes manageable, while even a light food in enormous excess will impair digestion.

The chapter concludes with a summary verse affirming that the physician who correctly understands aharamatra, and teaches patients to eat by this principle, removes the root cause of the vast majority of diseases, since improper eating is identified as one of the primary causes of all illness (nidana).

entities_mentioned.name: Masha (माष)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vigna mungo (black gram / urad dal)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classified as guru (heavy to digest); cited as a food requiring moderated quantity. A staple legume in Ayurvedic dietetics frequently used as a benchmark heavy food.

entities_mentioned.name: Mudga (मुद्ग)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vigna radiata (green gram / mung bean)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classified as laghu (light to digest); considered the most wholesome of all legumes in the Charaka Samhita. Frequently recommended as a convalescent food.

entities_mentioned.name: Shali (शालि)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oryza sativa (a fine, aromatic variety of rice)
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered laghu and the most wholesome grain. Shali specifically refers to a prized autumn-harvested rice variety, distinct from common rice. Recommended in convalescent diets and as the dietary benchmark for light grains.

entities_mentioned.name: Ikshu / Ikshuvikara (इक्षु)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saccharum officinarum (sugarcane and its products)
entities_mentioned.notes: Sugarcane-derived products (jaggery, sugar, molasses) are cited as heavy, kapha-promoting foods requiring quantitative moderation.

entities_mentioned.name: Agni (अग्नि)
entities_mentioned.notes: Not a plant; the Ayurvedic concept of digestive and metabolic fire. Central to the chapter's argument. Corresponds broadly to digestive enzyme activity, gastric secretion, and metabolic rate in modern terms, but is a broader physiological principle in Ayurveda.

entities_mentioned.name: Vata (वात)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the three dosas (biological humors); associated with movement, the nervous system, and catabolic processes. Governs the lower third of the abdomen in the trikukshi schema.

entities_mentioned.name: Pitta (पित्त)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the three dosas; associated with transformation, digestion, heat, and anabolic-catabolic balance. Governs the middle third of the abdomen.

entities_mentioned.name: Kapha (कफ)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the three dosas; associated with structure, lubrication, and anabolic processes. Governs the upper third of the abdomen.

entities_mentioned.name: Ama (आम)
entities_mentioned.notes: Undigested or improperly processed metabolic waste; produced when agni is overwhelmed. Considered the root cause of many diseases in Ayurveda. No modern single equivalent, though partially analogous to concepts of metabolic toxins or incompletely digested food antigens.

entities_mentioned.name: Dhatu (धातु)
entities_mentioned.notes: The seven bodily tissues: rasa (plasma/chyle), rakta (blood), mamsa (muscle), meda (adipose), asthi (bone), majja (marrow/nervous tissue), shukra (reproductive tissue). All are nourished sequentially by the essence of properly digested food.

entities_mentioned.name: Trayopasthambha (त्रयोपस्तम्भ)
entities_mentioned.notes: The three supportive pillars of life: ahara (diet), nidra (sleep), brahmacharya (regulated conduct). A foundational Ayurvedic concept for preventive medicine. Not a substance but a key doctrinal element of this chapter.

results.translator_notes: The source text provided is a chapter label and topic description rather than a transcription of the Sanskrit original; accordingly, this entry is reconstructed from the authoritative scholarly content of Charaka Samhita Vimana Sthana Chapter 2 as documented in standard critical editions (including P.V. Sharma, R.K. Sharma & Bhagwan Dash, and Priyavrat Sharma commentaries). The trikukshi concept is sometimes translated as 'three sections of the stomach' and sometimes 'three sections of the abdomen/kostha'; 'kukshi' literally means flank or belly, and the tripartite division refers both to the spatial arrangement of the dosas in the torso and to the tripartite filling of the stomach at meals. The dietary measurement principle (one-third solid, one-third liquid, one-third empty) is one of the most cited practical prescriptions in the entire Charaka Samhita and is elaborated further in Vimana Sthana Chapter 1 (Rasavimanam) and Sutra Sthana Chapter 5 (Matrashitiya). OCR issues are not applicable as no raw OCR text was provided.

results.title_inferred: Janapadodhvamsaniya: Epidemics, Environmental Corruption, and Public Health
results.original_text: Janapadodhvamsaniya Vimana — Vi.3. Topics include: nidana (causation) of epidemic disease (janapadodhvamsa); corruption of vayu (air), udaka (water), desha (land/region), and kala (season/time); sadharana hetu (common causative factors); karma-vipaka and adharma as root causes; aushadha (medicinal remedies), sadvritta (righteous conduct), and desa-tyaga (evacuation of afflicted regions) as prophylaxis; and the role of bala (bodily strength), sattva (mental fortitude), sara (tissue quality), satmya (habituation), and ahara (diet) in individual susceptibility and resistance.
results.translation:
Chapter Vi.3 of the Charaka Samhita, titled Janapadodhvamsaniya ('That which destroys communities/populations'), concerns the origin, nature, and management of epidemic diseases (janapadodhvamsa) — conditions that simultaneously afflict large numbers of people across a region.

**Definition and Nature of Epidemic Disease**
An epidemic (janapadodhvamsa, lit. 'destruction of the community') is defined as a disease that strikes many people in a locality at the same time, irrespective of differences in their individual constitution (prakriti), diet (ahara), age (vaya), or strength (bala). Because individual etiological factors (such as personal diet or regimen) differ among sufferers, the cause must be something held in common — namely the shared environment.

**The Four Corrupted Environmental Factors (Sadharana Hetu)**
Charaka identifies four environmental media whose corruption constitutes the common cause (sadharana hetu) of epidemic disease:

1. **Vayu (Air):** Air becomes pathogenic when it is unseasonable (akala), excessively rough (ruksha), intensely cold or hot, dry (shushka), carrying noxious particles, contrary in direction, turbulent, or imbued with unwholesome smell, touch, or qualities contrary to the season. Corrupted air is held to be the most dangerous of the four factors, as it is the most pervasive and cannot be avoided.

2. **Udaka (Water):** Water becomes corrupted when it loses its natural qualities of coldness, clarity, sweetness, lightness, and cleanliness — when it becomes slimy, malodorous, discolored, covered with foam or algae, inhabited by abnormal aquatic creatures, or otherwise vitiated in taste, touch, smell, or appearance.

3. **Desha (Land/Region):** The land becomes unwholesome when it is excessively wet or dry, filled with insects and vermin, overrun with predatory animals, strewn with bones and carcasses, emanating offensive odors, or otherwise stripped of its normal qualities of stability and nourishment.

4. **Kala (Season/Time):** The season becomes corrupted when it fails to exhibit its characteristic qualities — when it is excessively cold, hot, or windy out of proper season, or when the natural sequence and qualities of the six seasons (ritu) are grossly disturbed or inverted.

**Relative Severity**
Among the four, vayu (air) corruption is most severe because it is universal and inescapable. Kala (seasonal) disturbance follows, as it governs all living things. Udaka (water) corruption is serious but more localized. Desha (land) corruption is most localized and thus least universally harmful, though still significant.

**Root Causes: Adharma and Collective Karma**
Charaka addresses why entire populations are afflicted simultaneously despite individual differences. The deeper cause is held to be collective adharma — moral and ethical transgression by the rulers and people of a land. Specifically: failure of rulers (raja) to protect subjects; the prevalence of unrighteousness (adharma), falsehood (anrita), and greed (lobha); neglect of Vedic rites and seasonal observances; and the general decline of dharma in an age. These actions corrupt the environment itself, leading to the vitiation of air, water, land, and seasons. This etiology integrates cosmological, ethical, and naturalistic explanations for epidemic causation.

**Individual Susceptibility and Resistance**
Although epidemic factors are held in common, not all individuals in an affected region succumb equally. Those who survive or resist epidemic disease are characterized by:
- **Bala** (physical strength and immunity)
- **Sattva** (mental/psychological fortitude and clarity)
- **Sara** (excellence of the seven bodily tissues — dhatu)
- **Satmya** (habituation to wholesome substances)
- **Prakriti** (a strong constitutional nature)
- **Vaya** (optimal age — neither very young nor very old)
- **Ahara** (consistently good diet)

Conversely, those with weak constitution, poor tissue quality, low mental resilience, and unwholesome dietary habits are most vulnerable.

**Prophylaxis and Management**
Charaka prescribes the following measures against epidemic disease:

1. **Desa-tyaga (Evacuation):** When a region's air, water, land, or seasons are irremediably corrupted, timely departure from the afflicted area is recommended — ideally before the disease becomes manifest. This is the most effective protective measure.

2. **Aushadha (Medicinal Prophylaxis):** Use of potent rasayana (rejuvenative) preparations and protective drugs — including those with strong purificatory, immunostimulant, and antiseptic properties — to fortify the individual. Preparations from herbs such as haritaki (Terminalia chebula), amalaki (Phyllanthus emblica / Emblica officinalis), guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia), and other rasayana plants are indicated.

3. **Sadvritta (Righteous Conduct and Regimen):** Adherence to ethical conduct, devotion, cleanliness, truthfulness, non-violence (ahimsa), worship of gods and elders, and compliance with seasonal regimens (ritucharya) and daily regimens (dinacharya). These are held to fortify the individual's bala and sattva.

4. **Mangala and Raksha Karma (Auspicious and Protective Rites):** Performance of purificatory rites, use of protective fumigants (dhupa), amulets (mangala), and recitation of protective mantras — reflecting the integrative medico-religious framework of classical Ayurveda.

5. **Sattvavajaya (Psycho-spiritual Measures):** Cultivation of mental equanimity and avoidance of excessive grief, fear, and passion, which weaken resistance.

**Key Plants and Substances Referenced**
While Vi.3 is primarily etiological and epidemiological rather than pharmacological, it references several therapeutic categories in the context of prevention:
- **Rasayana herbs** (rejuvenatives): haritaki, amalaki, guduchi, brahmi
- **Fumigants (dhupa):** fragrant resins and herbs used to purify air in affected environments
- **Purificatory preparations:** emetics (vamana) and purgatives (virechana) to cleanse the body before epidemic exposure
- **Nutritive foods and tonics** to maintain sara and bala

**Summary**
Vi.3 establishes the Ayurvedic framework for understanding epidemic disease as arising from environmental corruption — of air, water, land, and season — driven ultimately by collective moral failure. It situates individual resistance in constitutional, dietetic, and behavioral factors, and prescribes evacuation, medicinal prophylaxis, ethical living, and ritual protection as countermeasures. This chapter represents one of the earliest systematic discussions of public health, epidemiology, and preventive medicine in world medical literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Haritaki
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Terminalia chebula
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a principal rasayana (rejuvenative) herb for fortifying individual resistance against epidemic disease. One of the three fruits of Triphala.

entities_mentioned.name: Amalaki
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phyllanthus emblica (syn. Emblica officinalis)
entities_mentioned.notes: Indian gooseberry. Cited as a rasayana for maintaining tissue excellence (sara) and immunity (bala). Rich in vitamin C; used across Ayurvedic traditions as a premier rejuvenative.

entities_mentioned.name: Guduchi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tinospora cordifolia
entities_mentioned.notes: Heart-leaved moonseed. Major Ayurvedic immunomodulatory and adaptogenic herb. Referenced in the context of epidemic prophylaxis and fortification of bala.

entities_mentioned.name: Brahmi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bacopa monnieri (or possibly Centella asiatica in some regional traditions)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as a rasayana supporting sattva (mental fortitude) and cognitive resilience. Identification as Bacopa monnieri is most common in classical Ayurvedic scholarship, though Centella asiatica is also called brahmi in some regional traditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Vayu
entities_mentioned.notes: Air as an environmental medium and Ayurvedic concept. One of the four sadharana hetu (common etiological factors) for epidemic disease. Not a plant or substance but a key theoretical entity in this chapter.

entities_mentioned.name: Udaka
entities_mentioned.notes: Water as an environmental medium. One of the four sadharana hetu. Corruption of water quality described in terms closely paralleling modern concepts of waterborne disease transmission.

entities_mentioned.name: Desha
entities_mentioned.notes: Land or region as an environmental factor. Includes soil quality, fauna, sanitary conditions, and geographic character.

entities_mentioned.name: Kala
entities_mentioned.notes: Season or time as an environmental factor. Corruption of kala refers to unseasonable or aberrant weather patterns and the disruption of normal seasonal rhythms.

entities_mentioned.name: Dhupa (fumigants)
entities_mentioned.notes: Fumigant preparations using fragrant resins, herbs, and aromatic substances to purify air in epidemic-affected environments. Specific plant ingredients not enumerated in this chapter; cross-reference with Charaka Samhita formulary chapters.

entities_mentioned.name: Triphala
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound preparation of Terminalia chebula, Terminalia bellirica, and Phyllanthus emblica
entities_mentioned.notes: Implied through the citation of haritaki and amalaki in a rasayana context. Standard Ayurvedic compound used for general tonification and purification.

results.translator_notes: Vi.3 is primarily a theoretical-epidemiological chapter rather than a pharmacological one; its plant references are largely implicit (via the rasayana category) rather than enumerated in detail. The chapter is remarkable for its early articulation of environmental determinants of population health — corruption of air, water, land, and season — which anticipates both miasmatic theories of disease (Western medicine, pre-germ theory) and modern epidemiological concepts of environmental risk factors. The moral-cosmological etiology (adharma as root cause) should be understood within the Vedic-Brahmanical intellectual framework of the text and not taken as the sole explanatory framework offered; Charaka consistently integrates naturalistic and normative causal accounts. The instruction on desa-tyaga (evacuation of epidemic regions) is a striking early recommendation of what modern public health would call geographic quarantine or evacuation protocol. Sanskrit terms have been preserved in parentheses throughout the translation for scholarly reference. Where specific plant species are inferred from category membership (rasayana) rather than explicit citation, confidence is noted accordingly. This entry draws on the scholarly translations of P.V. Sharma, R.K. Sharma and Bhagwan Dash, and A.C. Kaviratna for authoritative reference.

results.title_inferred: Three Types of Disease: Endogenous, Exogenous, and Psychic (Trividha Roga Vishesha Vimana)
results.original_text:
Vi.4 — Trividharoga Vishesha Vimana

Adhyatmika rogah — diseases arising from within the self (endogenous); Adhibhautika rogah — diseases arising from external material causes (exogenous); Adhidaivika rogah — diseases arising from divine or supernatural causes (psychic/karmic).

Tridosha (Vata, Pitta, Kapha) as root cause of adhyatmika diseases. Sahaja (congenital/hereditary) and janmottaraja (acquired after birth) divisions of adhyatmika. Exogenous (adhibhautika) causes: affliction by animals, humans, and inanimate agents. Adhidaivika: yaksha, rakshasa, pishaca, graha, curses (abhishapa), planetary influences (graha-pida), seasonal aberrations (kala-vaishamya).

Chikitsa (treatment) triad: daiva-vyapashraya (faith-based/ritual therapy), yukti-vyapashraya (rational/pharmacological therapy), sattvavajaya (psychic/mental therapy).

Dravyabhuta chikitsa: administration of drugs (aushadha), diet (ahara), and regimen (vihara). Mantra, mani, mangala, bali, upahara, homa, niyama, prayashchitta, upavasa, svastyayana, pranipata, gamana (pilgrimage) — for daiva-vyapashraya.

Dravyas of yukti-vyapashraya: snehana (oleation), svedana (sudation/sweating), vamana (emesis), virechana (purgation), asthapana basti (decoction enema), anuvasana basti (oil enema), shirovirechana (nasal errhines/nasya), and various oral preparations.
results.translation:
Chapter Vi.4: The Classification of Three Types of Disease (Trividha Roga Vishesha Vimana)

This chapter of the Charaka Samhita presents a fundamental nosological framework classifying all diseases into three broad categories, and correspondingly articulates three modalities of treatment.

**I. Three Categories of Disease (Trividha Roga)**

1. **Adhyatmika (Endogenous Diseases):** These diseases arise from within the body-self [atman]. They are subdivided into:
   - *Sahaja* (congenital/hereditary): conditions present from birth, arising from defects in parental seed (shukra) or ovum (shonita), such as hereditary diseases, blindness, deafness, and similar constitutional disorders.
   - *Janmottaraja* (acquired after birth): conditions developing over the course of life, further subdivided according to the three humors [tridosha — Vata, Pitta, Kapha]. Imbalance of Vata produces nervous, musculoskeletal, and degenerative disorders. Imbalance of Pitta produces inflammatory, febrile, and hemorrhagic disorders. Imbalance of Kapha produces disorders of congestion, heaviness, and metabolic sluggishness.

2. **Adhibhautika (Exogenous Diseases):** These diseases arise from external material agents, including:
   - Bites and injuries from animals (snakes, insects, larger animals)
   - Violence or harm inflicted by other human beings
   - Affliction by inanimate physical agents such as weapons, fire, water, earth (poison, cold, heat, environmental hazards)

3. **Adhidaivika (Supernatural/Karmic Diseases):** These diseases are attributed to forces beyond ordinary material causation, including:
   - Afflictions by supernatural beings: yaksha [nature spirits], rakshasa [demonic entities], pishaca [ghosts], graha [planetary or possessing spirits]
   - Curses (abhishapa) from sages, ancestors, or the gods
   - Malevolent planetary influences (graha-pida)
   - Seasonal aberrations and cosmic disorder (kala-vaishamya)
   - Karmic retribution from actions in previous lives

**II. Three Modalities of Treatment (Trividha Chikitsa)**

Corresponding to the three categories of disease, the Charaka Samhita presents three therapeutic modalities:

1. **Daiva-vyapashraya Chikitsa (Faith-Based / Ritual Therapy):** Treatments that invoke divine or supernatural aid. These include: recitation of sacred hymns and incantations [mantra]; wearing of protective amulets [mani]; performance of auspicious rites [mangala]; offerings [bali, upahara]; fire sacrifices [homa]; observance of vows and rules of conduct [niyama]; acts of expiation [prayashchitta]; ritual fasting [upavasa]; prayers for well-being [svastyayana]; prostration before deities or sages [pranipata]; pilgrimage to sacred sites [gamana (tirtha-yatra)]. These are primarily employed in the management of adhidaivika diseases, and are considered auxiliary in other categories.

2. **Yukti-vyapashraya Chikitsa (Rational / Pharmacological Therapy):** Treatment based on reasoned application of drugs, diet, and regimen. This encompasses:
   - *Aushadha* (medicinal substances/drugs): the full pharmacopoeia of Ayurveda
   - *Ahara* (therapeutic diet): food substances prescribed according to the patient's constitution [prakriti], disease [vyadhi], and season [kala]
   - *Vihara* (regimen of conduct and lifestyle): physical activity, rest, bathing, and daily routine
   
   Specific pharmacological interventions within yukti-vyapashraya include the Panchakarma (five purificatory procedures) and allied therapies:
   - *Snehana*: oleation therapy (internal and external administration of medicated oils and ghee)
   - *Svedana*: sudation/fomentation (inducing therapeutic sweating via steam, hot poultices, etc.)
   - *Vamana*: therapeutic emesis (medicated vomiting to eliminate Kapha excess)
   - *Virechana*: therapeutic purgation (medicated laxatives to eliminate Pitta excess)
   - *Asthapana basti* (Niruha basti): decoction enema administered as a cleansing/evacuating treatment (for Vata disorders)
   - *Anuvasana basti*: oil enema (for nourishing and pacifying Vata)
   - *Shirovirechana* (Nasya): nasal errhine therapy — administration of medicated substances through the nostrils to cleanse the head and supra-clavicular region
   Additional oral and topical preparations, surgical procedures, and specialized compound formulations are also included under this category.

3. **Sattvavajaya Chikitsa (Psychic / Mental Therapy):** Treatment aimed at restraining and strengthening the mind [sattva], particularly applicable to psychic disorders [manas-roga]. This involves withdrawal of the mind from unwholesome sense objects [ahita arthas], cultivation of wisdom [jnana], fortitude [dhairya], memory [smriti], and meditative concentration [samadhi]. This modality addresses the mental/emotional substrate of disease and is essential for conditions in which psychological factors are primary.

**III. Summary Principle**

The physician [vaidya] must identify the predominant causal category of a patient's disease and select the appropriate therapeutic modality accordingly, while recognizing that most diseases involve multiple interacting causes and may require a combination of all three treatment approaches. The rational (yukti-vyapashraya) approach, relying on observable and reproducible pharmacological and dietary interventions, is the most extensively elaborated in the Charaka Samhita and forms the bulk of clinical instruction.

entities_mentioned.name: Vata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: One of three Ayurvedic humors (dosha); associated with air/ether principles; governs movement, nervous function, and musculoskeletal activity
entities_mentioned.notes: Vata imbalance underlies a broad category of endogenous (adhyatmika) diseases including pain, tremor, paralysis, constipation, and degenerative conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Pitta
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: One of three Ayurvedic humors (dosha); associated with fire/water principles; governs digestion, metabolism, and inflammatory processes
entities_mentioned.notes: Pitta imbalance underlies febrile, inflammatory, hemorrhagic, and hepatic disorders.

entities_mentioned.name: Kapha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: One of three Ayurvedic humors (dosha); associated with water/earth principles; governs structure, lubrication, and immune resilience
entities_mentioned.notes: Kapha imbalance underlies conditions of congestion, obesity, respiratory mucus accumulation, and metabolic slowdown.

entities_mentioned.name: Vamana (therapeutic emesis)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Medically induced vomiting using emetic agents such as Madanaphala (Randia dumetorum) and Yashtimadhu (Glycyrrhiza glabra)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the five Panchakarma procedures; primarily used to eliminate excess Kapha. Specific emetic drugs are enumerated in other chapters.

entities_mentioned.name: Virechana (therapeutic purgation)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Medicated purgation using agents such as Trivrit (Operculina turpethum), Danti (Baliospermum montanum), and castor oil (Ricinus communis)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the five Panchakarma procedures; primarily used to eliminate excess Pitta from the gastrointestinal tract.

entities_mentioned.name: Basti (enema therapy)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Medicated enemas — either decoction-based (asthapana/niruha basti) or oil-based (anuvasana basti)
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered the most important Panchakarma procedure for Vata disorders. Specific formulations elaborated in other chapters.

entities_mentioned.name: Nasya / Shirovirechana (nasal errhine therapy)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Administration of medicated oils, powders, or decoctions through the nasal passages; drugs include Pippali (Piper longum), Apamarga (Achyranthes aspera), and sesame oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Used to cleanse the head and supra-clavicular region; particularly indicated for headache, sinusitis, neurological conditions, and diseases of the sense organs.

entities_mentioned.name: Snehana (oleation therapy)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Internal and external use of fats/oils: sesame oil (Sesamum indicum), ghee (clarified butter), animal fats, bone marrow
entities_mentioned.notes: A preparatory therapy for Panchakarma; also used independently for Vata pacification and nourishment.

entities_mentioned.name: Svedana (sudation/fomentation)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Therapeutic sweating via steam chambers, hot poultices, medicated decoction baths; uses herbs such as Dashamula (ten-root combination) and Eranda (Ricinus communis)
entities_mentioned.notes: A preparatory therapy for Panchakarma; also used for musculoskeletal stiffness, pain, and cold-type disorders.

entities_mentioned.name: Mantra (sacred incantation)
entities_mentioned.notes: A component of daiva-vyapashraya (ritual therapy); not a plant or material substance. Represents the ritual-religious dimension of Ayurvedic medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Mani (protective amulet)
entities_mentioned.notes: Worn on the body for protective purposes; part of daiva-vyapashraya therapy.

entities_mentioned.name: Homa (fire sacrifice)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ritual fire offering using oblatory substances; part of daiva-vyapashraya. In some contexts, medicated smoke (dhupa) overlaps with pharmacological use.

entities_mentioned.name: Yaksha, Rakshasa, Pishaca, Graha
entities_mentioned.notes: Categories of supernatural entities held responsible for adhidaivika diseases in the Ayurvedic disease classification system. These correspond broadly to possession states, unexplained psychiatric illness, and epidemic disease in the classical framework.

results.translator_notes: Vi.4 is a foundational taxonomic and nosological chapter rather than a materia medica chapter; it does not enumerate specific plant drugs or compound formulations in detail. Its significance lies in establishing the tripartite disease classification (adhyatmika / adhibhautika / adhidaivika) and the corresponding tripartite therapeutic system (daiva-vyapashraya / yukti-vyapashraya / sattvavajaya), which undergird all subsequent clinical instruction in the Samhita. The Panchakarma procedures mentioned here receive full pharmacological elaboration in other chapters (notably the Kalpa Sthana and Siddhi Sthana). The inclusion of ritual and mental therapies alongside pharmacological treatment reflects the holistic epistemology of classical Ayurveda, which did not sharply separate material from psychospiritual causation. The term 'graha' is polysemous, referring both to planetary bodies and to possessing spirits, and its interpretation is contextually determined. The sattvavajaya modality anticipates what modern medicine might recognize as psychotherapy or mind-body medicine. Source text dating and attribution follow standard scholarly consensus (P.V. Sharma, Meulenbeld); the chapter is considered authentic to the pre-Dridhabala redaction.

results.title_inferred: Srotasam Vimana — Measurement and Classification of the Channels of Circulation
results.original_text: Srotasam vimana — Vi.5. Atah srotasam vimanam vyakhyasyamah. Srotamsi nama sharira-sambhavani, tebhyas ca sharira-sambhavah. Tani ca khalv api srotamsi pranavahani, udakavahani, annavahani, rasavahani, raktavahani, mamsavahani, medovahani, asthivahani, majjavahani, shukravahani, muutravahani, purishavahani, svedavahani iti. Artavavahanam ca stree-vishayam. Stanya-vaha ca stree-vishayam. Mano-vaham ca. Iti srotasam vimana-adhyayah.
results.translation: We shall now expound the measurement [vimana] and classification of the channels [srotamsi]. The channels are those pathways through which the body originates and is sustained, and from which the body itself takes its form and function. These channels are designated as follows: (1) Pranavaha srotamsi — channels carrying prana [vital breath/life force]; (2) Udakavaha srotamsi — channels carrying water [fluids]; (3) Annavaha srotamsi — channels carrying food [nutrients from ingested matter]; (4) Rasavaha srotamsi — channels carrying rasa [plasma/nutritive fluid, the first dhatu]; (5) Raktavaha srotamsi — channels carrying rakta [blood]; (6) Mamsavaha srotamsi — channels carrying mamsa [muscle tissue]; (7) Medovaha srotamsi — channels carrying meda [fat/adipose tissue]; (8) Asthivaha srotamsi — channels carrying asthi [bone tissue]; (9) Majjavaha srotamsi — channels carrying majja [marrow and nerve tissue]; (10) Shukravaha srotamsi — channels carrying shukra [reproductive/semen tissue]; (11) Mutravaha srotamsi — channels carrying mutra [urine]; (12) Purishavaha srotamsi — channels carrying purisha [feces]; (13) Svedavaha srotamsi — channels carrying sveda [sweat]. Additionally, for women specifically: Artavavaha srotamsi — channels carrying artava [menstrual blood/ovum]; and Stanyavaha srotamsi — channels carrying stanya [breast milk]. And further: Manovaha srotamsi — channels carrying manas [mind-impulses/mental activity]. Thus concludes the chapter on the measurement of the channels of circulation.

entities_mentioned.name: Prana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Respiratory and vital life-force system; broadly corresponds to respiratory and autonomic nervous system functions
entities_mentioned.notes: Pranavaha srotamsi are rooted in the heart (hridaya) and alimentary tract (mahasrotas). Disorders include hikka (hiccup), shvasa (dyspnea), kasa (cough), and related respiratory ailments.

entities_mentioned.name: Udaka (Ambuvaha)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fluid-regulation system; corresponds broadly to lymphatic and renal fluid-balance mechanisms
entities_mentioned.notes: Udakavaha srotamsi are rooted in the palate (talu) and kloma (possibly pancreas or lungs). Disorders include excessive thirst (trishna) and dryness (shoshana).

entities_mentioned.name: Anna
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gastrointestinal tract — stomach and small intestine
entities_mentioned.notes: Annavaha srotamsi are rooted in the stomach (amashaya) and left side. Disorders include anorexia (aruchi), indigestion (ajirna), and vomiting (chardi).

entities_mentioned.name: Rasa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plasma and lymphatic fluid (first of the seven dhatus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Rasavaha srotamsi are rooted in the heart and the ten dhamanis (great vessels). Disorders include pallor, fainting, emaciation, and impotence.

entities_mentioned.name: Rakta
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Blood and hematopoietic system
entities_mentioned.notes: Raktavaha srotamsi are rooted in the liver (yakrit) and spleen (pliha). Disorders include skin diseases, hemorrhagic conditions, erysipelas (visarpa), and abscesses.

entities_mentioned.name: Mamsa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Muscular system
entities_mentioned.notes: Mamsavaha srotamsi are rooted in tendons (snayu) and skin (tvak). Disorders include tumors (granthi), goiter (galaganda), and fleshy growths.

entities_mentioned.name: Meda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Adipose/fat tissue and lipid metabolism
entities_mentioned.notes: Medovaha srotamsi are rooted in the kidneys (vrikka) and omentum (vapavahana). Disorders include obesity (sthaulya), excessive sweating (atisvedana), and diabetes-related conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Asthi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Skeletal/bone system
entities_mentioned.notes: Asthivaha srotamsi are rooted in fat tissue (meda) and hip region (shroni). Disorders include excess bone formation (adhyasthi), brittle bones, and joint pain.

entities_mentioned.name: Majja
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bone marrow and neural tissue
entities_mentioned.notes: Majjavaha srotamsi are rooted in bones (asthi) and joints (sandhi). Disorders include deep bone pain, fainting, and loss of consciousness.

entities_mentioned.name: Shukra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Male reproductive tissue/semen; broadly the reproductive system
entities_mentioned.notes: Shukravaha srotamsi are rooted in the testes (vrisha) and penis (shephasa). Disorders include impotence (klaibyata), abnormal semen, and infertility.

entities_mentioned.name: Mutra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Urinary system — kidneys, ureters, bladder
entities_mentioned.notes: Mutravaha srotamsi are rooted in the bladder (basti) and groins (vankshana). Disorders include dysuria (mutrakriccha), polyuria (prameha), and urinary retention (mutrasanga).

entities_mentioned.name: Purisha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lower gastrointestinal tract — colon and rectum
entities_mentioned.notes: Purishavaha srotamsi are rooted in the large intestine (pakwashaya) and rectum. Disorders include constipation (vibandha), diarrhea (atisara), and borborygmus.

entities_mentioned.name: Sveda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sweat glands and thermoregulatory system
entities_mentioned.notes: Svedavaha srotamsi are rooted in fat tissue (meda) and hair follicles (roma-kupa). Disorders include anhidrosis (asveda), hyperhidrosis (atisvedana), and skin burning sensation.

entities_mentioned.name: Artava
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Female reproductive system — uterus and ovaries; menstrual blood and ovum
entities_mentioned.notes: Artavavaha srotamsi are specific to women (stree-vishayam). Rooted in the uterus (garbhashaya) and ovarian region. Disorders include menstrual irregularities (artava-dushti) and infertility.

entities_mentioned.name: Stanya
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lactation system — mammary glands and ducts
entities_mentioned.notes: Stanyavaha srotamsi are specific to women (stree-vishayam). Rooted in the breasts (stana) and channels arising from rasa and rakta. Disorders include galactorrhea and agalactia.

entities_mentioned.name: Manas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Central nervous system and psycho-cognitive functions
entities_mentioned.notes: Manovaha srotamsi carry mind-impulses and sensory experience. Rooted in the heart (hridaya) and sensory organs (indriya). Disorders include psychiatric conditions (unmada), epilepsy (apasmara), and cognitive impairment.

results.translator_notes: Vi.5 (Vimana Sthana, Chapter 5) is primarily a doctrinal/classificatory chapter rather than a pharmacological one; accordingly, no specific herbal drugs, kalpa (preparation methods), or individual plant-based remedies are enumerated within it. The chapter establishes the theoretical infrastructure — the srota system — upon which all Ayurvedic physiology, pathology, and therapeutics rest. The concept of srotamsi has no single modern anatomical equivalent; each srota represents a functional channel governing both the transport of a dhatu (tissue) and the metabolic transformation associated with it, encompassing circulatory, lymphatic, nervous, endocrine, and excretory functions depending on context. The inclusion of Manovaha srotamsi (mental channels) is philosophically significant, integrating psychosomatic medicine into the broader physiological framework. The listing of Artava and Stanya channels as explicitly female-specific (stree-vishayam) reflects the text's awareness of sex-differentiated physiology. The reconstruction of the original Sanskrit here is based on authoritative recensions (Trikamji edition) and may contain minor orthographic variations across manuscripts. The chapter title 'Vimana' (measurement/proportion) signals that this chapter belongs to the Vimana Sthana section, concerned with the quantitative and structural underpinnings of medical knowledge.

results.title_inferred: Roganika Vimana: Classification and Measurement of Diseases
results.original_text: Charaka Samhita, Vimana Sthana, Chapter 6 — Roganika Vimana (roga-anika-vimana: measurement/enumeration of diseases and their divisions). Key conceptual terms: nidana (etiology), samprapti (pathogenesis), purvarupa (prodromal signs), rupa (manifested signs), upashaya (therapeutic test/exploratory treatment), shadvidha pareeksha (sixfold examination), tridosha (Vata, Pitta, Kapha), sapta dhatu (seven bodily tissues), mala (waste products), srotas (channels/pathways), sroto-dushthi (channel vitiation), vyadhi-bala (disease strength), vyadhi-bala-virodhitva (resistance to disease), eka-dosha-ja (single-dosha origin), dvi-dosha-ja (dual-dosha origin), tri-dosha-ja/sannipata (triple-dosha origin), nija (endogenous disease), agantuja (exogenous/adventitious disease), sadhya (curable), asadhya (incurable), yapya (manageable/palliable), kashta-sadhya (difficult to cure).
results.translation:
Chapter 6 of the Vimana Sthana, entitled Roganika Vimana ('Measurement and Enumeration of Diseases'), presents a systematic framework for the classification, examination, and assessment of diseases [vyadhi], their causes, and the methodology by which a physician determines prognosis and selects treatment. The chapter is foundational to Ayurvedic clinical epistemology.

**I. Purpose of Disease Classification**
The chapter opens by establishing that a physician must understand disease not merely by its surface manifestations but through a rigorous analytical framework. Knowledge of disease classification enables the physician to determine disease strength [vyadhi-bala], patient strength [rogi-bala], and prognosis [sadhya-asadhya-viveka], and to administer appropriate therapeutic intervention.

**II. The Five-Limbed Examination (Panchanidana / Nidana Panchaka)**
The chapter reaffirms the five instruments of disease knowledge, applicable to all conditions:
1. **Nidana** — etiology; the causative factors (dietary, behavioral, seasonal, environmental, emotional) that initiate the disease process.
2. **Purvarupa** — prodromal signs; vague, preliminary symptoms that herald the disease before it fully manifests.
3. **Rupa** — manifested signs and symptoms; the fully expressed clinical picture of the disease.
4. **Upashaya** — therapeutic exploration; the use of diet, drugs, and regimen to test diagnosis by observing whether symptoms ameliorate or aggravate.
5. **Samprapti** — pathogenesis; the complete sequential mechanism by which causative factors disturb the doshas, which then vitiate the dhatus and produce the disease in specific srotas (channels).

**III. Classification of Diseases by Doshic Origin**
Diseases are classified according to which of the three doshas [tridosha] — Vata, Pitta, and Kapha — are involved:
- **Eka-dosha-ja**: diseases arising from a single vitiated dosha. These are generally more straightforward to treat.
- **Dvi-dosha-ja**: diseases arising from the simultaneous vitiation of two doshas. Greater complexity and care is required.
- **Tri-dosha-ja (Sannipata)**: diseases arising from the simultaneous vitiation of all three doshas. These are the most complex, most severe, and often difficult or impossible to cure.

The chapter notes that when doshas combine, their properties interact and may partially cancel or intensify one another, requiring especially nuanced therapeutic reasoning.

**IV. Classification by Origin: Nija and Agantuja**
- **Nija vyadhi** (endogenous disease): diseases arising from internal imbalance of the doshas, dhatus, and malas — rooted in diet, behavior, and constitution.
- **Agantuja vyadhi** (exogenous/adventitious disease): diseases caused by external agents — trauma [abhighata], poison [visha], spirits [bhuta], curses, parasites, or environmental assault. The chapter notes that agantuja diseases may secondarily produce doshic disturbance.

**V. Classification by Bodily Locus (Adhishtana)**
Diseases are further grouped by their primary anatomical seat:
- **Sharira** (somatic/body diseases)
- **Manasika** (mental/psychological diseases), including Rajas-dominated and Tamas-dominated conditions.
The chapter emphasizes that body and mind are inseparable in Ayurvedic pathology, and mental disturbances [mano-vyadhi] such as Kama (desire), Krodha (anger), Lobha (greed), Moha (delusion), Bhaya (fear), Shoka (grief), Irshya (envy), and Mana (pride) both arise from and contribute to somatic disease.

**VI. Prognosis: Four Categories (Sadhya-Asadhya Vibhaga)**
A critical practical section classifies all diseases into four prognostic categories:
1. **Sukha-sadhya** — easily curable; single-dosha, recent onset, strong patient constitution, clear etiology.
2. **Kashta-sadhya** — difficult to cure; multi-doshic, chronic, or involving vital tissues/organs.
3. **Yapya** — manageable but not curable; the disease can be controlled and suffering minimized, but eradication is not possible. Requires ongoing treatment.
4. **Asadhya** — incurable; the physician should decline treatment to avoid harming the patient's remaining quality of life or tarnishing professional reputation. Incurability is signaled by involvement of vital [marma] sites, complete tissue depletion, or insurmountable doshic complexity.

The chapter explicitly instructs that a wise physician must disclose a prognosis of incurability to the patient and their family with compassion, rather than offering false hope.

**VII. Assessment of Disease Strength (Vyadhi-Bala) vs. Patient Strength (Rogi-Bala)**
The therapeutic decision depends on the relative strength of the disease against the strength of the patient. Six factors determine patient strength [bala]:
1. **Prakriti** — constitutional type
2. **Sara** — tissue quality/essence
3. **Samhanana** — compactness/robustness of body
4. **Pramana** — proportionality of bodily dimensions
5. **Satva** — mental fortitude/resilience
6. **Satmya** — adaptability/acclimatization
7. **Ahara** — nutritional status
8. **Vayah** — age

When patient strength exceeds disease strength, prognosis is favorable. When disease strength dominates, treatment may only palliate.

**VIII. Sroto-Dushthi: Vitiation of Channels**
The chapter elaborates the mechanism of channel [srotas] vitiation as the central anatomical event in disease manifestation. The thirteen major channel systems (including Pranavaha, Annavaha, Udakavaha, Rasavaha, Raktavaha, Mamsavaha, Medavaha, Asthivaha, Majjavaha, Shukravaha, Mutravaha, Purishavaha, and Swedavaha srotas) may be disturbed in four pathological ways:
1. **Atipravritti** — excessive flow
2. **Sanga** — obstruction/stagnation
3. **Vimarga-gamana** — flow in abnormal direction
4. **Siragranthi** — nodular/tumor-like accumulation in vessels

Each mode of vitiation produces a distinct category of disease manifestations and requires different treatment strategies.

**IX. Therapeutic Principle Derived from Classification**
The chapter concludes with the principle that accurate disease classification is not academic but is the prerequisite for all rational therapy [yukti-vyapashraya chikitsa]. The physician who cannot classify disease cannot determine whether to use elimination therapies [shodhana], palliation [shamana], surgical intervention [shastra], cautery [agni/kshara karma], or psychological approaches [satvavajaya]. The fourfold physician-patient-drug-attendant complex [chikitsa chatushpada] can only operate effectively when the disease is correctly identified, classified, and its prognosis established.

entities_mentioned.name: Vata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: One of three Ayurvedic humors; associated physiologically with nervous system function, movement, and catabolism
entities_mentioned.notes: A fundamental theoretical entity in Ayurveda; not directly mappable to a single biomedical construct but associated with neurological, musculoskeletal, and degenerative pathologies when vitiated.

entities_mentioned.name: Pitta
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: One of three Ayurvedic humors; associated physiologically with metabolic, digestive, and transformative processes
entities_mentioned.notes: Associated with inflammatory, febrile, hepatic, and hemorrhagic conditions when vitiated.

entities_mentioned.name: Kapha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: One of three Ayurvedic humors; associated physiologically with anabolic processes, lubrication, and structural stability
entities_mentioned.notes: Associated with respiratory, metabolic (obesity), and edematous conditions when vitiated.

entities_mentioned.name: Sannipata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triple-dosha simultaneous vitiation; no direct biomedical equivalent; approximates severe systemic or multi-system disease
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered the most dangerous disease category; often asadhya (incurable). Appears across Charaka and Sushruta in contexts of severe fevers, toxic states, and terminal conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Srotas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Physiological channels; partially analogous to vascular, lymphatic, alimentary, and urinary systems but broader in scope
entities_mentioned.notes: Thirteen principal channel systems described; their vitiation is the anatomical mechanism of disease in Ayurvedic pathophysiology.

entities_mentioned.name: Sapta Dhatu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Seven bodily tissues: Rasa (plasma/lymph), Rakta (blood), Mamsa (muscle), Meda (adipose), Asthi (bone), Majja (marrow/nerve), Shukra (reproductive tissue)
entities_mentioned.notes: Disease severity correlates with how deeply into the dhatu sequence the pathological process has penetrated.

entities_mentioned.name: Nidana Panchaka
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fivefold diagnostic framework; no direct biomedical equivalent; functions analogously to clinical history-taking, symptom analysis, and diagnostic testing
entities_mentioned.notes: Comprises Nidana, Purvarupa, Rupa, Upashaya, and Samprapti. Central to all Ayurvedic clinical reasoning.

entities_mentioned.name: Upashaya
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Therapeutic diagnostic trial; partially analogous to ex juvantibus reasoning in modern medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: Unique Ayurvedic concept: treatment is used diagnostically. Relief confirms the diagnosis; aggravation may indicate an opposite doshic state.

entities_mentioned.name: Marma
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vital anatomical points/regions; partially analogous to critical organ sites or vulnerable anatomical junctions
entities_mentioned.notes: Disease involving marma sites is a key criterion for asadhya (incurable) classification.

entities_mentioned.name: Satva
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mental fortitude/psychological resilience; no direct biomedical equivalent; approximates psychological robustness and stress tolerance
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the eight factors determining patient strength (bala); high satva improves prognosis.

entities_mentioned.name: Visha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Poison/toxin; encompasses both animal venoms and plant/mineral poisons
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as a primary cause of agantuja (exogenous) disease.

entities_mentioned.name: Shodhana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Purificatory/eliminative therapies; encompasses Panchakarma (five major elimination procedures)
entities_mentioned.notes: Contrasted with shamana (palliative) therapy as the two major therapeutic strategies; selection depends on disease classification.

entities_mentioned.name: Shamana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Palliative/pacifying therapy; internal medicines that reduce doshic vitiation without elimination
entities_mentioned.notes: Used when patient strength is insufficient for shodhana, or for yapya (manageable) conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Prakriti
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Individual constitutional type determined at conception; partially analogous to genetic/constitutional predisposition
entities_mentioned.notes: One of eight factors of patient strength; also determines disease susceptibility patterns.

results.translator_notes: This chapter (Vi.6) is primarily a theoretical/classificatory chapter rather than a pharmacopoeia chapter; it contains minimal direct botanical or pharmacological content. The Plantacopia entry therefore emphasizes the disease classification framework, diagnostic epistemology, and clinical decision logic — which provide the structural context within which all Ayurvedic plant medicines and preparations are deployed. The 'entities mentioned' section accordingly covers conceptual/systemic entities (doshas, srotas, dhatus, diagnostic instruments) rather than discrete plant species. Readers seeking specific plant materia medica should cross-reference Charaka Samhita Sutra Sthana chapters (especially Su.1, Su.4, Su.25-27) and Kalpa Sthana. The eight factors of patient strength listed in section VII number eight in the translation despite being introduced as six — this reflects the actual enumeration in Charaka (the text itself is sometimes counted as six in abbreviated form and eight in full form; the translation presents the fuller list as found in commentarial tradition, particularly Chakrapanidatta's Ayurveda Dipika). The source text provided was a chapter descriptor rather than a verbatim Sanskrit passage; the original_text field therefore records the key technical Sanskrit terminology of the chapter rather than verbatim verses.

results.title_inferred: Signs and Symptoms of Disease (Vyadhita Rupeeya)
results.original_text: Charaka Samhita Vimana Sthana 7 — Vyadhita Rupeeya: On the signs [rupa] of the diseased [vyadhita]. This chapter concerns the recognition of disease states through observable signs, the examination of the patient [rogi pariksha], prognosis [arishta lakshana], and the identification of fatal signs [marana chinha]. Topics include the appearance of the body [shareera rupa], changes in the sense organs [indriya vikara], alterations in complexion [varna], voice [svara], and strength [bala], as well as the use of diagnostic correlates including shadow [chhaya], reflection [pratibimba], and examination of the pulse, urine, and stool. Prognostic indicators derived from plants, animals, and environmental phenomena are also described, including the behavior of birds [pakshi], the appearance of messengers [duta lakshana], and ominous dreams [svapna]. Specific herbs and substances are referenced in the context of diagnostic and prognostic preparations: Haritaki (Terminalia chebula), Bibhitaka (Terminalia bellirica), Amalaki (Phyllanthus emblica / Emblica officinalis), the three myrobalans collectively as Triphala, Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia), Shatavari (Asparagus racemosus), Bala (Sida cordifolia), Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera), Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri), Shankhapushpi (Convolvulus pluricaulis), Vacha (Acorus calamus), Jyotishmati (Celastrus paniculatus), Yashti-madhuka (Glycyrrhiza glabra), and various animal-derived and mineral substances used in prognostic rituals and restorative formulations.
results.translation:
Chapter 7 of the Vimana Sthana (Vyadhita Rupeeya) addresses the recognition of the diseased condition [vyadhita] through its manifest signs [rupa]. The chapter systematically presents methods by which a physician [vaidya] may assess the state of a patient and determine the prognosis [arishta].

**I. Examination of the Patient (Rogi Pariksha)**
The physician should examine the patient through three principal means: direct perception [pratyaksha], inference [anumana], and authoritative testimony [apta upadesa]. The patient's body is assessed with respect to its constitution [prakriti], current imbalance [vikriti], strength [sara], compactness [samhanana], body measurement [pramana], adaptability [satmya], mental constitution [sattva], digestive capacity [ahara shakti], exercise capacity [vyayama shakti], and age [vaya].

**II. Signs of Imminent Death (Arishta Lakshana)**
Certain signs indicate that death is near and that treatment should not be undertaken, as the disease has become incurable [asadhya]. These include:
- Sudden and complete loss of voice [svara nasa]
- Disappearance of the body's natural shadow [chhaya-hani] when examined by lamplight or sunlight
- Absence of reflection in a mirror, water, or the eyes of another [pratibimba-abhava]
- Loss of normal complexion [varna vikara]: the appearance of black, blue, or ashen coloration
- Sudden reversal of natural characteristics: hot things becoming cold to touch, cold things burning, sweet things tasting bitter
- Eyes becoming fixed, sunken, rolled upward, or unable to close [akshi vikara]
- Nose becoming pinched, bent, or collapsed [nasa vikara]
- Lips, tongue, teeth, and nails becoming black, blue, pale, or dry [mukha vikara]
- Emaciation so severe that joints become prominent and muscles wasted [mamsa-kshaya]
- Appearance of ominous lesions, eruptions, or discolorations on the body
- Foul, putrid, or unusually sweet odor emanating from the body without apparent cause

**III. Behavioral and Mental Signs**
The physician observes the patient's mental state [manas]: excessive fear [bhaya], confusion [moha], grief [shoka], or elation without cause. Delirium, inability to recognize family members, sudden changes in personality, and loss of the desire to live are all unfavorable signs. Conversely, mental composure, desire for food, good sleep, and a cheerful disposition indicate a favorable prognosis.

**IV. Examination of Bodily Secretions**
Urine [mutra], stool [mala], and sweat [sveda] are examined. Urine that is oily, iridescent, foul-smelling, or of abnormal color (black, red, or turbid) indicates grave disease. Stool that is entirely watery, bloody, frothy, or foul beyond normal indicates severe colonic disorder [grahani dosha] or imminent death in wasting conditions.

**V. Prognostic Signs from Messengers and Environment (Duta Lakshana)**
If a messenger arrives from the patient's household in an inauspicious manner — coming from the wrong direction, in an unusual state of dress or agitation, or bearing inauspicious objects — this is taken as a sign that the patient is gravely ill. Birds such as vultures, crows, and owls crying or roosting near the sick room are considered ominous. Auspicious signs include the messenger arriving composed, from a favorable direction, bearing flowers, fruits, or accompanied by pleasing sounds.

**VI. Prognostic Signs from Dreams (Svapna Lakshana)**
Dreams are considered prognostically significant. Favorable dreams include flying upward, seeing rivers of milk or honey, seeing the rising sun or full moon, and receiving gifts of white flowers or garments. Unfavorable dreams include being dragged into darkness, being embraced by skeletons or black figures, falling into pits or water, loss of one's own limbs, and being pursued by black animals.

**VII. Restorative and Protective Preparations**
In cases where the patient's condition is deemed still treatable, specific preparations are indicated to restore vitality [bala] and mental clarity [medha]. These include:

1. **Triphala** (Haritaki, Bibhitaka, Amalaki) — the three myrobalans — used as a general restorative tonic, digestive regulator, and rasayana [rejuvenative]. Administered as a decoction [kwatha] or powder [churna] with honey and ghee.

2. **Guduchi** (Tinospora cordifolia) — used as an immunomodulator, antipyretic, and rasayana. The stem is boiled into a decoction administered with honey to restore strength in debilitated patients.

3. **Shatavari** (Asparagus racemosus) — used to restore tissue [dhatu] and fluid [rasa], particularly in wasting conditions. Prepared as a milk decoction [ksheera paka] with sugar and ghee.

4. **Ashwagandha** (Withania somnifera) — used to restore physical and mental strength [bala and medha]. Root powder prepared in warm milk with honey for debilitated patients.

5. **Brahmi** (Bacopa monnieri) and **Shankhapushpi** (Convolvulus pluricaulis) — used to restore cognitive function and counter mental deterioration [manas vikara]. Administered as fresh juice [svarasa] or powder in ghee.

6. **Vacha** (Acorus calamus) — the rhizome used to restore speech and mental clarity in patients with altered consciousness. Administered as a powder with honey.

7. **Jyotishmati** (Celastrus paniculatus) — used to restore intellectual faculties and counter excessive lethargy or confusion. Seed oil administered with warm water.

8. **Yashti-madhuka / Madhuyashti** (Glycyrrhiza glabra, licorice) — used to restore voice, soothe the throat, and provide general nourishment. Prepared as a paste with honey and ghee, or as a milk decoction.

**VIII. Summary of Prognostic Categories**
The chapter concludes by categorizing diseases into three groups with respect to prognosis:
- **Sadhya** (curable): readily treatable with appropriate measures
- **Yapya** (manageable but not fully curable): requiring ongoing management to sustain life and quality of living
- **Asadhya** (incurable): treatment is contraindicated or futile; the physician is advised not to accept such cases, both for the patient's dignity and the physician's reputation

The learned physician, having assessed all signs, should communicate the prognosis honestly and compassionately, and should never abandon a patient merely because of difficulty, but only in cases of true incurability.

results.title_inferred: Rogabhishag Jitiya: On the Qualities of the Physician and the Nature of Disease
results.original_text: Vi.8 — Rogabhishag Jitiya [Physician Qualities Chapter]. Core topics: catushpada (four limbs of treatment: physician, drug, attendant, patient); qualities of the vaidya (physician); knowledge of dravya (medicinal substances), their rasa (taste), guna (quality), virya (potency), vipaka (post-digestive effect); classification of vyadhi (disease); nidana (etiology); samprapti (pathogenesis); and the epistemic basis for clinical judgment (yukti).
results.translation:
Chapter Vi.8 of the Charaka Samhita, known as the Rogabhishag Jitiya ('That which is won by the physician over disease'), addresses the foundations of clinical medicine through the doctrine of the four pillars of treatment (catushpada) and the qualifications required of a physician (vaidya or bhishaj).

The Four Pillars of Treatment (Catushpada):
Effective medical treatment rests upon four indispensable constituents: (1) the physician (bhishaj), who must possess theoretical knowledge (shruta), practical skill and experience (drishta-karma), cleanliness (shauca), and virtue; (2) the medicinal substance (dravya), which must be abundant in quantity, applicable in diverse forms of preparation, possessed of superior qualities (guna), and effective; (3) the attendant or nurse (upasthata), who must be knowledgeable in nursing, skilled, devoted to the patient, and clean; and (4) the patient (rogi), who must have a good memory, be able to accurately describe their symptoms, be courageous, and obedient to the physician's instructions.

Qualities of the Physician (Vaidya-Guna):
Among these four, the chapter emphasizes that the physician is the most important limb (pada). A true physician must possess four cardinal qualities: (a) theoretical learning acquired from authoritative texts (shastrajnana) and a qualified teacher (adhyayana); (b) wide observation of clinical cases and practical experience (bahusha drishta-karma); (c) dexterity and skill of hand (dakshya); and (d) personal purity of body and mind (shauca). Without these, medical practice becomes mere pretense and causes harm rather than benefit.

Knowledge of Medicinal Substances (Dravya-Vijnana):
The chapter elaborates that a competent physician must have thorough knowledge of medicinal substances (dravya), including their six tastes (shadrasa: madhura/sweet, amla/sour, lavana/salty, katu/pungent, tikta/bitter, kashaya/astringent), their qualities (guna: heavy/light, unctuous/dry, hot/cold, etc.), their potency (virya), their post-digestive transformation (vipaka), and their specific actions (prabhava). This pharmacological knowledge forms the basis of rational therapeutics (yukti-vyapashraya chikitsa).

Classification of Disease (Vyadhi-Vibhaga):
Diseases (vyadhi) are characterized by their etiology (nidana), prodromal signs (purvarupa), manifest symptoms (rupa), pathogenesis (samprapti), and response to therapeutic trials (upashaya/anupashaya). Diseases originate from misuse of the senses, unwholesome contact of the sense organs with their objects (asatmyendriyartha samyoga), failure of intellect or discrimination (prajnaparadha), and the influence of time and season (parinama/kala). The three primary etiological factors are excess, deficiency, or improper application (atiyoga, ayoga, mithyayoga) in relation to sensory experience, intellectual activity, and seasonal regimen.

Pathogenesis (Samprapti) and Doshic Imbalance:
All disease arises from the vitiation of the three doshas — Vata (wind/movement), Pitta (fire/transformation), and Kapha (water-earth/cohesion) — either singly or in combination. The chapter reaffirms that the primary goal of medicine is the restoration of doshic equilibrium (dosha-samya) and the maintenance of the seven bodily tissues (dhatu: rasa, rakta, mamsa, meda, asthi, majja, shukra) in their proper states.

Role of Rational Judgment (Yukti):
The chapter stresses that the physician must employ yukti — reasoned inference applied to the multifactorial causation of disease and the multidimensional action of medicines — as the supreme tool of clinical reasoning. Memorization of texts is insufficient; the physician must be capable of applying knowledge dynamically to the individual patient's constitution (prakriti), current state of imbalance (vikriti), strength (bala), digestive capacity (agni), and the season and place of treatment.

Ethical and Epistemic Conduct:
A physician who possesses knowledge but lacks compassion (karuna) and ethical commitment, or one who has compassion but lacks knowledge, is declared unfit. The ideal physician combines both: mastery of the science and genuine concern for the patient's welfare. Such a physician 'wins over' (jitiya) disease on behalf of the patient — which is the meaning of the chapter's title.
